citation: Collins, C. L., McKenzie, L. B., Ferketich, A. K., Andridge, R., & Xiang, H. (2016). Dental injuries sustained by high school athletes in the United States, from 2008/2009 through 2013/2014 academic years. Dental Traumatology, 32(2), 121–127.
extraction_notes: Aggregate row across all reported sports. Mouthguard use rate is computed from abstract: 72.5% of injured athletes were not wearing a MG, so 27.5% were — note this is MG use AT TIME OF INJURY (not population prevalence). 95.9% of worn MGs were self-fitted. Per-sport rates by competition vs practice not extracted at aggregate level (see other rows).

citation: Collins, C. L., McKenzie, L. B., Ferketich, A. K., Andridge, R., & Xiang, H. (2016). Dental injuries sustained by high school athletes in the United States, from 2008/2009 through 2013/2014 academic years. Dental Traumatology, 32(2), 121–127.
extraction_notes: Rate during competition only. Abstract reports RR competition vs practice = 3.1 (95% CI 2.3–4.0).

citation: Collins, C. L., McKenzie, L. B., Ferketich, A. K., Andridge, R., & Xiang, H. (2016). Dental injuries sustained by high school athletes in the United States, from 2008/2009 through 2013/2014 academic years. Dental Traumatology, 32(2), 121–127.

citation: Labella, C. R., Smith, B. W., & Sigurdsson, A. (2002). Effect of mouthguards on dental injuries and concussions in college basketball. Medicine and Science in Sports & Exercise, 34(1), 41–44.
extraction_notes: Subgroup: athletes using custom-fitted mouthguards. AE = 8663 of 70,936 total (12.2% of AE). Injury count not directly in abstract; back-calc from rate × AE = ~1 injury. Confirm with full text.

citation: Labella, C. R., Smith, B. W., & Sigurdsson, A. (2002). Effect of mouthguards on dental injuries and concussions in college basketball. Medicine and Science in Sports & Exercise, 34(1), 41–44.
extraction_notes: Subgroup: athletes NOT using custom-fitted mouthguards. AE = 62,273 (= 70,936 total − 8,663 MG users). Injury count not directly in abstract; back-calc from rate × AE ≈ 42 injuries. Confirm with full text. Abstract reports P<0.05 for MG vs non-MG dental injury rate difference.

citation: Stewart, G. B., Shields, B. J., Fields, S., Comstock, R. D., & Smith, G. A. (2009). Consumer products and activities associated with dental injuries to children treated in United States emergency departments, 1990–2003. Dental Traumatology, 25(4), 399–405.
injury_type_raw: dental injuries (unspecified type) treated in US EDs
rate_denominator_raw: per 100,000 children <18 yrs per year (population denominator)
extraction_notes: Population-denominator surveillance, NOT athlete-exposure denominator — rate_per_1000_ae left empty (incompatible denominators). NEISS counts all ED-treated dental injuries, not only sport-related; sport subset is described qualitatively in the abstract (highest in 13–17 group, baseball and basketball leading). Source's age range 0–17 partially overlaps the 5–22 inclusion floor; per PROTOCOL §3.1 the source qualifies (majority within scope).

citation: Stewart, G. B., Shields, B. J., Fields, S., Comstock, R. D., & Smith, G. A. (2009). Consumer products and activities associated with dental injuries to children treated in United States emergency departments, 1990–2003. Dental Traumatology, 25(4), 399–405.

injury_type_raw: dental injuries (unspecified type) treated in US EDs
extraction_notes: Children with primary dentition (<7y). Abstract states >50% of all dental injuries in the dataset; products were predominantly home structures/furniture (non-sport). Numerical counts per dentition stage require full text.

citation: Stewart, G. B., Shields, B. J., Fields, S., Comstock, R. D., & Smith, G. A. (2009). Consumer products and activities associated with dental injuries to children treated in United States emergency departments, 1990–2003. Dental Traumatology, 25(4), 399–405.
